does: This paper introduces Dynamic Boundary Evaluation (DBE), a new evaluation framework that moves beyond fixed benchmarks by adaptively locating each language model's performance boundary—where pass probability is near 50% under random sampling. The method constructs a calibrated item bank covering safety, capability, and truthfulness dimensions, validated across nine reference LLMs with per-item difficulty labels. It develops Skill-Guided Boundary Search (SGBS), an algorithm requiring only API-level access that efficiently finds boundary items for target models. The evaluation protocol places new LLMs on a unified ability scale and adaptively expands the evaluation set when models fall outside the bank's difficulty coverage, addressing ceiling and floor effects that mask true capability gaps in traditional fixed benchmarks.
connects: This work extends decades of psychometric research on adaptive testing and item response theory, translating these principles into the LLM evaluation setting where ceiling and floor effects plague fixed benchmarks like MMLU and HellaSwag. It builds on recent critiques of static evaluation paradigms and responds to the growing recognition that benchmark saturation obscures meaningful differences between models. The paper connects to prior work on computerized adaptive testing, which similarly locates examinee ability through strategic item selection, and draws on emerging methods for efficient model evaluation under API constraints. It also engages with safety evaluation research that has struggled with fixed refusal thresholds, extending that literature toward dynamic, comparative assessment.
enables: DBE establishes infrastructure for continuous, adaptive evaluation as new models emerge, enabling more precise capability tracking across model families and versions. The unified ability scale supports rigorous comparison of models otherwise difficult to rank directly, opening avenues for studying scaling laws with respect to per-category skill rather than aggregate benchmark scores. This framework enables fine-grained analysis of safety properties like over-refusal versus under-refusal using principled difficulty metrics, potentially informing better RLHF alignment strategies. Future work can leverage the approach to construct adaptive benchmarks that remain challenging and discriminative as model capabilities advance, and to study how capabilities cluster—whether safety and capability share a single dimension or require separate measurement scales.
